content.text:
Based on the full chart image, I can analyze the Meats (red dots) directly.

**Key formula:** To maximize weight (grams) with $5, I need to minimize **cost per gram**:

> Cost per gram = (Y-axis: cost per 100 cal) × (X-axis: cal per gram) / 100

Let me read approximate coordinates for each meat from the chart:

| Meat | Cal/gram (X) | Cost/100cal (Y) | Cost/gram (Y×X/100) | Grams for $5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chicken breast | ~1.1 | ~$0.80 | ~$0.0088 | **~568g** |
| Chicken wing | ~2.0 | ~$0.45 | ~$0.009 | ~556g |
| Turkey | ~1.5 | ~$0.65 | ~$0.00975 | ~513g |
| Canned tuna | ~0.9 | ~$1.15 | ~$0.0104 | ~483g |
| 80% ground beef | ~3.5 | ~$0.40 | ~$0.014 | ~357g |
| Pork belly | ~4.5 | ~$0.45 | ~$0.0203 | ~247g |
| Ribeye steak | ~2.5 | ~$1.25 | ~$0.03125 | ~160g |

Chicken breast sits at the lowest caloric density (~1.1 cal/g) among the affordable meats, and while its cost per 100 cal (~$0.80) is moderate, its low caloric density means you get relatively more grams per dollar.

<think>
Bob wants to maximize weight with $5. The cost per gram = (cost/100cal) × (cal/gram) / 100. Looking at all Meats dots, Chicken breast (x≈1.1 cal/g, y≈$0.80/100cal) gives cost per gram ≈ $0.0088/g → about 568g for $5. Chicken wing (x≈2.0, y≈$0.45) gives $0.009/g → 556g. Chicken breast wins with the lowest product of x×y among meats.
</think>

<answer>
Chicken breast. With $5, Bob should buy chicken breast from the Meats category to maximize weight. Chicken breast has a low caloric density (~1.1 cal/gram) and a moderate cost (~$0.80 per 100 calories), giving a cost per gram of roughly $0.0088/g, yielding approximately 568 grams for $5 — the highest among all meats on the chart.
</answer>
